<doc>
    <assembly>

    <members>
        <member name="T:System.IdHelpers">
            <summary>
            Provides helper methods for monotonic ID generation.
            </summary>
        </member>
        <member name="M:System.IdHelpers.CreateMonotonicId(System.DateTimeOffset)">
            <summary>
            Creates a strictly monotonically increasing identifier string using 64-bit timestamp ticks
            and a 64-bit counter, formatted as a 32-character hexadecimal string (GUID-like).
            </summary>
            <param name="timestamp">The timestamp to embed in the identifier.</param>
            <returns>A new strictly monotonically increasing identifier string.</returns>
            <remarks>
            <para>
            This method creates a 128-bit identifier composed of two 64-bit values:
            - High 64 bits: <see cref="P:System.DateTimeOffset.Ticks"/> from the timestamp
            - Low 64 bits: A globally monotonically increasing counter
            </para>
            <para>
            The resulting string is strictly monotonically increasing when compared lexicographically,
            which is required for keyset pagination to work correctly. Unlike <c>Guid.CreateVersion7</c>,
            which uses random bits for intra-millisecond uniqueness, this implementation guarantees
            strict ordering for all identifiers regardless of when they were created.
            </para>
            </remarks>

        <member name="M:System.IdHelpers.CreateMonotonicId(System.DateTimeOffset)">
            <summary>
            Creates a strictly monotonically increasing identifier string using 64-bit timestamp ticks
            and a 64-bit counter, formatted as a 32-character hexadecimal string (GUID-like).
            </summary>
            <param name="timestamp">The timestamp to embed in the identifier.</param>
            <returns>A new strictly monotonically increasing identifier string.</returns>
            <remarks>
            <para>
            This method creates a 128-bit identifier composed of two 64-bit values:
            - High 64 bits: <see cref="P:System.DateTimeOffset.Ticks"/> from the timestamp
            - Low 64 bits: A globally monotonically increasing counter
            </para>
            <para>
            The resulting string is strictly monotonically increasing when compared lexicographically,
            which is required for keyset pagination to work correctly. Unlike <c>Guid.CreateVersion7</c>,
            which uses random bits for intra-millisecond uniqueness, this implementation guarantees
            strict ordering for all identifiers regardless of when they were created.
            </para>
            </remarks>

data.content:
Here's the complete project at `McpHttpServer/`. The key pieces:

**Setup (lines 5–13):**
- `AddMcpServer()` registers core MCP services
- `.WithHttpTransport()` enables the Streamable HTTP transport
- `.WithTools<MyTools>()` / `.WithResources<MyResources>()` register your handlers
- `app.MapMcp()` maps the MCP endpoint at `/` (pass a string like `"/mcp"` to customize)

**Tools** — use `[McpServerToolType]` on the class and `[McpServerTool]` on each method. Parameters are automatically exposed in the JSON schema.

**Resources** — use `[McpServerResourceType]` on the class and `[McpServerResource]` on each method:
- **Static resource** (`config://app`) — fixed URI, returns JSON config data
- **Resource template** (`config://env/{name}`) — parameterized URI with `{name}` mapped to the method parameter

**Packages:** `ModelContextProtocol` + `ModelContextProtocol.AspNetCore` (v1.2.0).

I verified all endpoints respond correctly: `tools/list`, `tools/call`, `resources/list`, `resources/templates/list`, and `resources/read`.
